all_items: ['Missed Period: The most common indicator, though stress or health factors can also cause delays.', 'Breast Changes: Tender, swollen, or tingling breasts, along with darkening of the areolas, often occur 2–3 days after conception.', 'Implantation Bleeding & Cramping: Light pink or brown spotting and mild cramping can occur 10–14 days after conception when the egg attaches to the uterus.', 'Fatigue: High levels of the hormone progesterone can make you feel extremely tired.', 'Nausea: Known as "morning sickness," this can occur at any time of day, typically starting 2-8 weeks after conception.', 'Frequent Urination: Increased blood flow and hormonal changes cause the bladder to fill faster.', 'Heightened Sense of Smell and Taste: Many experience a metallic taste or new sensitivities to odors.']

query: what are the symptoms of pregnancy
all_items: ['Missed Period: Usually the most common sign, especially if your cycle is regular.', 'Tender/Swollen Breasts: Caused by hormonal changes, making breasts feel heavy or sensitive.', 'Fatigue: Intense tiredness is common, often due to higher levels of the hormone progesterone.', 'Nausea with or without Vomiting: Known as morning sickness, this can actually occur at any time of the day or night.', 'Frequent Urination: Increased blood flow and fluid cause the kidneys to process more waste.', 'Implantation Bleeding/Cramping: Light pink or brown spotting may occur when the embryo attaches to the uterus.', 'Darkening of the Areola: The area around the nipples may darken and become more sensitive.', 'Food Aversions or Cravings: Sudden disinterest in favorite foods or intense cravings.', 'Heightened Sense of Smell: Increased sensitivity to odors.', 'Bloating and Constipation: Increased hormones slow down the digestive system.', 'Mood Swings: Rapid emotional shifts.', 'Headaches or Backaches: Mild headaches or lower back pain can occur.']

query: what are the signs of a miscarriage
all_items: ['Vaginal Bleeding: This is the most common sign, which may start as light brown, coffee-ground-like spotting before becoming heavy, bright red bleeding.', 'Abdominal/Pelvic Cramping: Painful cramps in the lower abdomen that may be dull, sharp, or persistent.', 'Back Pain: Pain in the lower back that can range from mild to severe.', 'Passing Tissue: Passing greyish or clot-like tissue through the vagina.', 'Decreased Pregnancy Symptoms: A sudden disappearance or reduction in pregnancy signs like breast tenderness or nausea.', 'Fluid Discharge: A sudden gush of clear or pink fluid.', 'Not All Bleeding Means Miscarriage: Vaginal bleeding is common in the first trimester, particularly around week 12, and does not always indicate a pregnancy loss.', 'Missed Miscarriage: Sometimes, a miscarriage occurs without any symptoms (known as a "missed miscarriage" or "silent miscarriage"), and is only discovered during a routine scan.', 'Evaluation Needed: Even if you think you have passed the entire pregnancy, you should be evaluated by a healthcare professional.']

query: what to expect in first trimester
all_items: ['Extreme Fatigue: Progesterone increases can cause severe tiredness.', 'Morning Sickness: Nausea and vomiting are common, not just in the morning.', 'Breast Changes: Breasts may become tender, swollen, or heavier.', 'Frequent Urination: Increased blood flow and a growing uterus press on the bladder.', 'Mood Swings: Similar to PMS, hormonal surges can cause mood fluctuations.', 'Constipation and Heartburn: Digestion slows down due to high progesterone levels.', 'Food Cravings/Aversions: Changes in taste can lead to specific cravings or disgust for certain foods.', 'Weight Changes: Minimal weight gain (approx. 3–6 pounds) is expected.', 'Weeks 1–4: The fertilized egg implants, and the amniotic sac and placenta begin forming.', 'Week 5: The neural tube, which becomes the brain and spinal cord, closes.', 'Week 6-7: The heart starts beating, and buds that will become limbs appear.', 'Week 12: The fetus looks like a tiny human, with all vital organs in place.', 'First Prenatal Visit: Usually scheduled between weeks 8–12 for health history and physical exams.', 'Screening Tests: Urine tests, blood tests (blood type, Hb levels), and sometimes a dating ultrasound.', 'Lifestyle Adjustments: Take folic acid, stop smoking/alcohol, limit caffeine, and begin eating small, frequent meals to manage nausea.', 'Vaginal bleeding or severe cramping.', 'Severe vomiting or inability to keep liquids down.', 'Fever over $38^{\\circ }\\text{C}$ ( $100^{\\circ }\\text{F}$).', 'Painful urination.']

all_items: ['Raw or Undercooked Meat and Poultry: Raw beef, poultry, or pork can contain bacteria like Salmonella and E. coli.', 'Raw or Undercooked Fish & Shellfish: Avoid sushi, sashimi, raw oysters, and scallops to prevent infections.', 'High-Mercury Fish: Avoid shark, swordfish, king mackerel, and tilefish.', 'Deli Meats and Hot Dogs: Deli meats (ham, turkey, bologna), hot dogs, and cold cuts should be avoided unless they are cooked until steaming hot to prevent Listeria.', 'Unpasteurized Dairy & Soft Cheeses: Avoid raw milk, soft cheeses (brie, camembert, feta, blue cheese) unless labeled "pasteurized".', 'Raw Eggs: Avoid runny eggs, homemade mayonnaise, mousse, and raw cookie dough due to Salmonella risks.', 'Raw Sprouts: Avoid raw alfalfa, clover, radish, and mung bean sprouts.', 'Unwashed Produce: Unwashed fruits and vegetables, especially leafy greens, can contain parasites like Toxoplasma.', 'Refrigerated Smoked Seafood: Often labeled as lox or nova-style, this should be avoided unless cooked into a dish.', 'Alcohol: There is no known safe amount of alcohol during pregnancy, so avoid it completely.', 'Caffeine: Limit intake to less than 200 mg per day, roughly one 12-ounce cup of coffee.', 'Unpasteurized Juices and Cider: These can contain harmful bacteria.', 'Avoid raw or undercooked ready-made meals and pâté.', 'Ensure all food is stored and prepared under hygienic conditions.', 'Consult a doctor or dietitian for personalized nutrition advice.']

all_items: ['High Blood Pressure: Often detected at prenatal visits.', 'Severe Headache: A new headache that does not go away with medication.', 'Vision Changes: Blurry vision, seeing spots, flashing lights, or sensitivity to light.', 'Upper Abdominal Pain: Severe pain under the ribs, usually on the right side, sometimes mistaken for heartburn or gallbladder issues.', 'Sudden Swelling (Edema): Rapid swelling of the face, hands, or feet.', 'Rapid Weight Gain: Often caused by fluid retention.', 'Shortness of Breath: Caused by fluid in the lungs.', 'Nausea or Vomiting: Occurring suddenly in the second half of pregnancy.', 'Reduced Urine Output: Urinating less frequently.', 'Severe nausea/vomiting.', 'Sudden weight gain (>2–5 pounds in a week).', 'Severe pain in the upper right belly or shoulder.', 'Mental status changes, such as confusion or agitation.']

all_items: ['Labor stalls: The labor process is slow or obstructed (dystocia).', 'Fetal distress: The baby is not receiving enough oxygen or has an abnormal heart rate.', "Baby's position: The baby is in a breech (feet down) or shoulder position.", 'Placenta issues: The placenta covers the cervix (placenta previa) or detaches.', 'Multiple pregnancies: Carrying twins, triplets, or more.', 'Health conditions: The mother has active herpes, high blood pressure, or a previous C-section.', 'Anesthesia: Most women are awake, receiving spinal or epidural anesthesia to numb the lower body. General anesthesia (being asleep) is used in rare emergencies.', 'Incision: A doctor usually makes a horizontal incision across the lower abdomen (the "bikini cut"), followed by an incision in the uterus.', 'Delivery & Recovery: The baby is delivered within minutes. The entire surgery takes about 15–30 minutes, though preparation takes longer.', 'Recovery time: A hospital stay usually lasts 3 to 5 days, with full recovery taking up to 6 weeks.']

query: how to prepare for labor and delivery
all_items: ['Exercise: Perform prenatal yoga, squats, pelvic tilts, and hip circles to strengthen muscles and increase pelvic flexibility.', 'Perineal Massage: Massaging the perineum in the final weeks can prepare tissue for stretching.', 'Pelvic Floor Exercises: Practice Kegels to help with control during pushing.', 'Stay Active: Regular walking and swimming help improve stamina for labor.', 'Childbirth Education Classes: Understand the stages of labor and pain management options.', 'Birth Plan/Preferences: Outline preferences for pain relief, environment, and immediate postpartum care, but remain flexible.', 'Relaxation Techniques: Practice meditation, breathing exercises, or visualization to manage fear and pain.', 'Support Team: Hire a doula or designate a support person, such as a partner or family member.', 'Hospital Bag Checklist: Pack clothes for you and the baby, toiletries, snacks, ID, and comfort items (music, pillows).', 'Install Car Seat: Ensure the car seat is properly installed by 37 weeks.', 'Hospital Plan: Know the route to the hospital and when to call the doctor (e.g., water breaks, regular contractions).', 'Stay Hydrated & Nourished: Eat light, nutritious meals and drink water to maintain energy.', 'Move: Walk around, sway, or use a birth ball to help with comfort and positioning.', 'Rest: Try to rest or sleep during early labor when contractions are manageable.']

all_items: ['Persistent Sadness/Hopelessness: Feeling empty, numb, or very sad most of the day.', 'Intense Irritability and Rage: Feeling angry, easily frustrated, or "on edge".', "Severe Anxiety and Panic Attacks: Worrying excessively or feeling paralyzed by fear, sometimes focused on the baby's safety.", 'Worthlessness and Guilt: Feeling like a bad mother, or feeling intense shame, guilt, or inadequacy.', 'Loss of Pleasure: No longer enjoying activities that used to bring joy.', 'Difficulty Bonding: Feeling distant, disconnected, or unattached to the baby.', 'Insomnia or Extreme Fatigue: Inability to sleep even when the baby is sleeping, or sleeping far too much.', 'Appetite Changes: Significant loss of appetite or eating much more than usual.', 'Withdrawal: Isolating from friends and family.', 'Cognitive Issues: Trouble focusing, thinking clearly, or making decisions.', 'Physical Aches: Unexplained headaches, body aches, or stomach pain.']

all_items: ['Irregular Menstrual Cycles: Cycles that are consistently too long (35+ days), too short (under 21 days), or unpredictable make it difficult to calculate ovulation.', 'Absent Periods (Amenorrhea): Missing periods for 3 months or more may indicate a failure to ovulate.', 'Painful or Heavy Periods: Intense cramping, heavy bleeding, or chronic pelvic pain may signal conditions like endometriosis or fibroids.', 'Hormonal Imbalance Symptoms: Unexplained weight gain, severe acne, hair loss, or excessive hair growth (hirsutism) on the chin, chest, or back may indicate disorders like PCOS.', 'Pain During Sex: Consistent pain (dyspareunia) during intercourse can be linked to conditions that affect fertility, such as pelvic inflammatory disease or endometriosis.', 'Unusual Nipple Discharge: Milky discharge unrelated to breastfeeding can suggest high levels of prolactin, which can affect ovulation.', 'Multiple Miscarriages: Experiencing multiple consecutive miscarriages should be evaluated by a specialist.', 'Under 35 and have been trying for 1 year.', '35 or older and have been trying for 6 months.']

all_items: ['Eat Antioxidant-Rich Foods: Berries, walnuts, and leafy greens combat free radicals that damage egg and sperm quality.', 'Incorporate Healthy Fats: Omega-3 fatty acids found in salmon, chia seeds, and walnuts support fertility.', 'Consume More Plant-Based Protein: Beans, lentils, and nuts are excellent sources to reduce the risk of ovulatory infertility.', 'Focus on Complex Carbs: Reduce refined carbohydrates (white bread/rice) to manage insulin levels, which can help regulate ovulation, particularly for those with PCOS.', 'Increase Fiber: Fiber-rich foods (whole grains, fruits, vegetables) help eliminate excess waste and balance hormones.', 'Maintain a Healthy Weight: Being under- or overweight can cause hormonal imbalances and hinder ovulation.', 'Reduce Alcohol and Smoking: Smoking can significantly decrease fertility, and alcohol consumption should be reduced.', 'Manage Stress: High stress affects hormones that are vital for conceiving.', 'Exercise in Moderation: Regular, moderate exercise is recommended, as excessive intense exercise can decrease fertility in both men and women.', 'Track Ovulation: Timing sexual intercourse 3-4 days before ovulation and up to one day after is optimal.', 'Increase Folate Intake: Leafy greens, citrus fruits, and fortified cereals are high in folic acid, crucial for embryo development.', 'Reduce Heat Exposure for Men: Avoid hot tubs, saunas, and tight underwear, which can lower sperm count.', 'Trans Fats: Processed foods, fried items, and margarine should be avoided.', 'High-Mercury Fish: Avoid high-mercury seafood, which can affect fertility.', 'Smoking/Vaping: Both are linked to reduced fertility.']

all_items: ['Meaning: "In vitro" means "in glass" (laboratory dish), representing fertilization outside the human body.', 'Purpose: It is used to treat infertility, including conditions like blocked fallopian tubes, low sperm count, or unexplained infertility.', "Usage: It can use a couple's own eggs and sperm or, in some cases, donor sperm, eggs, or embryos.", 'Stimulation (Super Ovulation): Medications are given to stimulate the ovaries to produce multiple eggs rather than the single egg that typically matures each month.', 'Egg Retrieval: A minor surgical procedure is performed to remove the eggs from the ovarian follicles.', 'Insemination/Fertilization: The retrieved eggs are mixed with sperm in a laboratory setting to facilitate fertilization.', 'Embryo Culture: The fertilized eggs develop into embryos over several days in an incubator.', 'Transfer: One or more embryos are transferred directly into the uterus (womb) to implant and grow.', 'Age: Younger patients typically have higher success rates.', 'Cause of Infertility: The underlying reason for needing IVF.', 'Embryo Quality: The health of the developed embryos.']

all_items: ['Procedure: A thin, flexible tube (catheter) is inserted through the cervix into the uterus to deposit washed and concentrated sperm. The procedure is fast, usually painless (similar to a Pap smear), and done in a clinic.', 'Purpose: To increase the number of sperm that reach the fallopian tubes, increasing the likelihood of pregnancy.', 'Timing: It is timed with ovulation, which may be monitored naturally or induced using fertility medications.', 'Best For: Individuals with unexplained infertility, mild male factor infertility, cervical issues, or those using donor sperm.', 'IUI vs. IVF: IUI is less invasive and less expensive than In Vitro Fertilization (IVF). Unlike IVF, where fertilization happens in a lab, IUI relies on fertilization occurring inside the body.', 'Success Rates: Success depends on factors like age and the reason for infertility, with success rates often around 20% per cycle.', 'Low sperm count or reduced sperm motility.', 'Cervical mucus issues that hinder sperm.', 'Ejaculation dysfunction.', 'Unexplained infertility.', 'Single individuals or same-sex couples using donor sperm.']

all_items: ['Anovulation: The primary issue is irregular or absent ovulation, meaning eggs are not released regularly.', 'Hormonal Imbalance: High levels of androgens (male hormones) and insulin resistance can interfere with egg development.', 'Pregnancy Risks: Women with PCOS have a higher risk of gestational diabetes, miscarriage, and pregnancy complications.', 'Lifestyle Changes: For those with obesity, a 5-10% weight loss can restore regular ovulation and improve fertility.', 'Medication (First-Line): Letrozole is often preferred to encourage ovulation, followed by clomiphene citrate.', 'Metformin: Used to improve ovulation rates by treating insulin resistance.', 'Assisted Reproductive Technology (ART): If medicines are ineffective, IVF has high success rates for women with PCOS.', 'Age Factor: Women with PCOS may have a better ovarian reserve as they age, with many finding their cycles become more regular in their 30s.', 'Medical Guidance: It is essential to consult a reproductive endocrinologist for a personalized plan to manage irregular periods and trigger ovulation.']

all_items: ['Initial Consult & Screening: Doctors assess your ovarian reserve via blood tests and ultrasounds to determine the appropriate hormone dosage.', 'Ovarian Stimulation (10-12 days): You self-administer hormone injections to stimulate the ovaries to mature multiple eggs rather than the single egg normally produced monthly. Regular monitoring visits (ultrasounds/blood tests) check follicle growth.', 'Trigger Shot: When follicles are mature, a final "trigger shot" (hCG or Lupron) is given to finalize egg maturation.', 'Egg Retrieval (10-30 mins): A fertility specialist performs an outpatient, ultrasound-guided procedure while you are under sedation to retrieve the eggs via a needle passed through the vaginal wall.', 'Freezing (Vitrification): Embryologists immediately freeze the mature eggs using vitrification, an ultra-rapid cooling technique that prevents ice crystals from damaging the cell.', 'Success Rates: The best outcomes occur when eggs are frozen under age 35, although it is common up to 38.', 'Number of Eggs: Generally, 15-20 mature eggs are recommended for a high probability of a live birth.', 'Storage & Usage: Eggs can be stored for many years. When ready, they are thawed, fertilized with sperm via ICSI (intracytoplasmic sperm injection), and the resulting embryo is transferred to the uterus.', 'Side Effects: Mild cramping, bloating, or mood swings from medications are common, but serious complications are rare.']

all_items: ['Hormonal IUD (e.g., Mirena, Kyleena): Releases progestin, effective for 3–8 years.', 'Copper IUD (Paragard): Hormone-free, uses copper to prevent pregnancy, lasts up to 10–12 years.', 'Implant (Nexplanon): A small rod placed under the skin of the upper arm, effective for 3–5 years.', 'Combined Oral Contraceptives (The Pill): Daily pill containing estrogen and progestin.', 'Progestin-Only Pill (Mini-pill): Daily pill suitable for those who cannot take estrogen.', 'Patch (Xulane): Worn on the skin and replaced weekly for 3 weeks, with 1 week off.', 'Vaginal Ring (NuvaRing): A flexible ring inserted into the vagina, replaced monthly.', 'Injection (Depo-Provera): A shot given by a doctor every 3 months.', 'External Condoms: Male condoms (latex or non-latex) are the only method that also helps prevent STIs.', 'Internal Condoms: Female/internal condoms placed inside the vagina.', 'Diaphragm/Cap: A silicone cup inserted into the vagina, usually with spermicide.', 'Sponge/Spermicide: Devices or foams that kill or block sperm.', 'Vasectomy: A male sterilization procedure that prevents sperm from leaving the body.', 'Tubal Ligation/Occlusion: A female procedure where fallopian tubes are blocked or cut.', 'Emergency Copper IUD: The most effective form, inserted within 5 days.', 'Emergency Pills (Levonorgestrel or Ulipristal acetate): Taken within 3–5 days.', 'Fertility Awareness-Based Methods: Tracking ovulation (temperature, cervical mucus) to avoid sex on fertile days.']

all_items: ['Hormonal IUDs (e.g., Mirena, Kyleena, Liletta, Skyla): These release small amounts of progestin, which thickens cervical mucus to block sperm and thins the uterine lining. They last between 3 to 8 years.', 'Copper IUD (ParaGard): This is non-hormonal, wrapped in copper, which acts as a spermicide to prevent fertilization. It can last up to 10–12 years.', 'Extremely Effective: With less than 1 pregnancy per 100 women in the first year of use, they are among the most effective contraceptives available.', 'Long-Acting & Reversible: They provide long-term protection, but fertility returns immediately upon removal.', 'Emergency Contraception: The copper IUD can be used as highly effective emergency contraception if inserted within 5 days of unprotected sex.', 'Menstrual Benefits: Hormonal IUDs often make periods lighter and less painful, while the copper IUD may increase bleeding.', 'Insertion Procedure: A doctor or nurse inserts the IUD during a quick office visit; some discomfort or cramping is common.', 'No STI Protection: IUDs do not protect against sexually transmitted infections (STIs), so condoms should still be used.', 'Side Effects: Potential side effects include irregular spotting for the first few months or changed period intensity.']

all_items: ['Copper IUD (Non-Hormonal): Heavier, longer, and more painful periods are common, particularly in the first 3–6 months, along with spotting between periods.', 'Hormonal IUD (Progestin): Irregular bleeding or spotting in the first few months. Other possible effects include breast tenderness, acne, headaches, and mood changes. However, after 3–6 months, many users experience very light periods or no periods at all.', 'Insertion Pain: Cramping or backaches during or immediately after insertion.', 'Persistent Spotting: Irregular bleeding that often subsides within 3-6 months.', 'Expulsion: The IUD may come out, which is most common in the first 3 months.', 'Perforation: In rare cases, the IUD can pierce the uterine wall.', 'Infection: A small risk of pelvic infection exists shortly after insertion.', 'Severe or persistent abdominal pain.', 'A fever or chills.', 'Inability to feel the IUD strings.', 'Unusually heavy bleeding.']

all_items: ['Mechanism of Action: The high dose of levonorgestrel temporarily inhibits ovulation. It may also alter the cervical mucus, making it harder for sperm to pass.', 'When It Fails: If ovulation has already occurred, Plan B will likely not work. It is not effective if a fertilized egg has already implanted in the uterus.', 'What It Is Not: Plan B is not the abortion pill (mifepristone/misoprostol). It cannot terminate an existing pregnancy or harm a developing fetus.', 'Effectiveness and Timing: It reduces the risk of pregnancy by up to 89% if taken within 72 hours. Efficacy decreases as time passes, but it can still be taken up to 5 days after sex.', 'Limitations: It may be less effective for individuals with a body weight over 165 lbs. It is intended for backup use, not as a primary form of birth control.', 'Menstrual Cycle Changes: Your next period might be heavier, lighter, earlier, or later than normal.', 'When to Call a Doctor: If your period is more than a week late, you may be pregnant, so you should take a test.', 'Repeated Use: It is safe to use more than once, but it is less effective and more expensive than regular contraception.']

all_items: ['Effectiveness: It is nearly 100% effective for permanent contraception, with a failure rate of only about 15 per 10,000 couples in the first year.', "Procedure: Usually performed in a doctor's office in 15–20 minutes under local anesthetic.", 'Recovery: Quick recovery, often involving a few days of mild discomfort and restricted physical activity.', 'Post-Op Requirements: The procedure is not effective immediately; it usually takes about 3 months or a specific number of ejaculations for the semen to be completely clear of sperm.', 'No Impact on Sexual Performance: It does not change testosterone levels, sexual desire, erection quality, or the ability to experience orgasm.', 'No Protection Against STIs: It does not prevent the transmission of sexually transmitted infections (STIs).', 'Permanence: It is considered irreversible, as reversals are complex and not always successful.', 'Incision Method: The surgeon makes one or two small cuts in the scrotum to access the tubes.', 'No-Scalpel Method: The surgeon uses a special tool to make a tiny puncture rather than a cut, resulting in less bleeding and faster healing.', 'Vasectomy: A much simpler, safer, and cheaper procedure compared to female tubal ligation (sterilization).']

all_items: ['Highly Effective: Over 99% effective, providing, according to the Cleveland Clinic, reliable, long-term contraception.', 'Permanent Solution: Ideal for individuals sure they do not want future pregnancies, eliminating the need for daily or temporary birth control.', 'Non-Hormonal: Does not affect your natural hormone levels, menstrual cycle, or mood.', 'Reduced Ovarian Cancer Risk: Studies suggest it may lower the risk of developing ovarian cancer, especially if the entire tube is removed.', 'Immediate Results: Protects against pregnancy immediately after the procedure.', 'Permanent/Hard to Reverse: Meant to be permanent, and reversal procedures are difficult, expensive, and not guaranteed to work.', 'No Protection Against STIs: Does not offer any protection against sexually transmitted infections (STIs).', 'Surgical Risks: As with any surgery, risks include bleeding, infection, and reaction to anesthesia, as noted by the NCCRM.', 'Regret: There is a possibility of regret later, particularly for women who undergo the procedure at a younger age.', 'Ectopic Pregnancy Risk: If a pregnancy occurs, it is more likely to be an ectopic pregnancy (outside the uterus), which is dangerous.', 'Alternative Options: Long-acting reversible contraceptives (LARCs) like IUDs or implants are actually more effective than tubal ligation and do not require surgery, explains Everyday Health.', 'Partnership Considerations: Vasectomies are often less invasive, more effective, and easier to reverse than tubal ligation.', 'Salpingectomy vs. Ligation: Complete removal of the tubes (salpingectomy) provides higher protection against ovarian cancer than just tying them (ligation).']

query: abortion pill how does it work
all_items: ['Mifepristone: This first pill is usually taken at a clinic or at home. It blocks the hormone progesterone, which is necessary to maintain the pregnancy. Without progesterone, the uterine lining breaks down, and the pregnancy cannot continue.', 'Misoprostol: Taken 24 to 48 hours after the first pill, these tablets are taken orally or vaginally. They cause the uterus to contract and empty, causing cramping and bleeding, which expels the pregnancy tissue.', 'Process Duration: The process often takes several hours. Most people pass the pregnancy tissue within 4–5 hours of taking the second medicine, though it can take longer.', 'Symptoms: Intense cramping, heavy bleeding, and passing blood clots are expected. Other symptoms may include nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, fever, or chills.', 'Recovery: Bleeding and spotting may continue for several weeks, which is normal.', 'Follow-Up: A follow-up appointment or test is sometimes necessary to ensure the abortion is complete.', 'Effectiveness: The combined regimen is over 90% effective.', 'Misoprostol-Only: In some cases, only misoprostol is used (often 12 pills taken over several hours), which is also effective in ending early pregnancies.', 'Restriction: These medications are typically available by prescription and are highly restricted in some regions.']

query: what to expect after an abortion
all_items: ['Bleeding: Often lasts 1–2 weeks, though it can be intermittent or start 3-5 days later. Medication abortion typically causes more bleeding than procedural.', 'Cramping: Mild to moderate cramping is normal for a few days to two weeks.', 'Clots: Passing small blood clots is normal.', 'Pregnancy Symptoms: Nausea and breast tenderness should fade within 1–2 weeks.', 'Pain Relief: Use ibuprofen or acetaminophen to manage discomfort.', 'Rest: Plan to rest for at least 24 hours afterward.', 'Sanitary Products: Use pads, not tampons, for the first few days to monitor bleeding and reduce infection risk.', 'Sexual Activity: Avoid sexual intercourse for 1–2 weeks, or until bleeding stops, to prevent infection.', 'Fertility: Fertility returns almost immediately, so contraception can be used right away.', 'Soaking through two or more maxi pads per hour for two consecutive hours.', 'Pain or cramps that do not improve with medication.', 'Fever of $100.4^{\\circ}F$ ( $38^{\\circ}C$) or higher.', 'Foul-smelling vaginal discharge.']

query: what are the symptoms of HPV
all_items: ['Genital Warts: The most common sign, these are single or multiple warts that can be itchy, tender, or painful, often appearing on the vulva, penis, anus, or groin.', 'Warts on Other Areas: Sometimes appearing in the throat (recurrent respiratory papillomatosis), mouth, or lips.', 'No Visible Symptoms: Most people with HPV do not know they have it because they never develop symptoms.', 'Pre-cancerous Cell Changes: High-risk HPV may show no symptoms until it has progressed to cancer, which is why routine screenings like Pap smears are essential.', 'Unusual growths, bumps, or sores in the genital or anal area.', 'Bleeding between periods or after sex.', 'Warts that are painful, bleed, or grow.', 'Wart Strains vs. Cancer Strains: The strains that cause genital warts (often 6 and 11) are low-risk and generally do not cause cancer.', 'Long-Term Risks: HPV can take 15–20 years to cause cervical cancer, which is why monitoring and vaccinations are critical for prevention.']

all_items: ['Sexual Contact: Unprotected anal or vaginal sex is the main transmission method, with anal sex carrying the highest risk. The virus enters the body through the lining of the vagina, penis, rectum, or mouth.', 'Sharing Needles/Syringes: Sharing drug injection equipment (“works”) with someone living with HIV can pass the virus directly into the bloodstream.', 'Perinatal (Mother-to-Child): HIV can be passed from mother to baby during pregnancy, childbirth, or breastfeeding.', 'Blood Contact: While rare due to screening, transmission can occur through contaminated blood transfusions or organ transplants.', 'Viral Load: People with HIV who take antiretroviral therapy (ART) and maintain an undetectable viral load cannot transmit HIV to their sexual partners, a concept known as Undetectable = Untransmittable (U=U).', 'Other STIs: The presence of other sexually transmitted infections can increase the risk of transmission.', 'Kissing, hugging, or shaking hands.', 'Sharing toilets, food, or drinks.', 'Saliva, sweat, or tears.', 'Mosquito or other insect bites.', 'PrEP (Pre-Exposure Prophylaxis): A daily medicine for HIV-negative people to prevent infection.', 'PEP (Post-Exposure Prophylaxis): Medicine taken within 72 hours after a potential exposure.', 'Condoms: Correct and consistent use reduces the risk of transmission.']

all_items: ['Synonyms/Types: Common forms include daily tablets (e.g., Truvada, Descovy) or long-acting injections (e.g., Apretude).', 'Usage Examples: Taken daily by individuals at high risk, including those with HIV-positive partners, people sharing needles, or those not using condoms.', 'Effectiveness: When taken as prescribed, it reduces HIV risk from sex by ~99% and injection drug use by at least 74%.', 'Limitations: PrEP does not protect against other sexually transmitted infections (STIs) and must be used consistently to be effective.', 'Daily Pills: A single tablet containing tenofovir and emtricitabine (or similar).', 'Injectables: Long-acting injections are administered either every two months or twice yearly.', 'Preparation: It takes about 7 days for daily pills to reach maximum protection against HIV exposure from anal sex, and up to 20 days for vaginal sex/drug use.']

all_items: ['Symptoms: Severe pelvic pain (often during periods or intercourse), chronic fatigue, heavy menstrual bleeding, and gastrointestinal issues.', 'Locations: Commonly found on the ovaries, ligaments supporting the uterus, and the peritoneum (lining of the pelvis/abdomen).', 'Diagnosis: Primarily diagnosed via surgical visualization (laparoscopy), though ultrasound is also used.', 'Treatment: While no cure exists, symptoms are managed with medication (hormonal therapies), pain relief, and surgery to remove endometrial growths.', 'Prevalence: Affects approximately 10% of women of reproductive age worldwide, as well as transgender and non-binary individuals who menstruate.']

all_items: ['Irregular periods: Missed, irregular, or very light/heavy periods.', 'Androgen excess: High male hormones leading to hirsutism (excess facial/body hair), severe acne, and male-pattern baldness.', 'Weight gain: Difficulty losing weight, particularly around the abdomen.', 'Ovarian changes: Enlarged ovaries with multiple small, fluid-filled sacs (cysts).', 'Skin issues: Darkening of the skin (acanthosis nigricans) or skin tags.', 'Fertility issues: Difficulty becoming pregnant.', 'Mental health: Increased risks of anxiety, depression, and eating disorders.', 'Lifestyle Interventions: Losing 5–10% of body weight through a healthy, low-calorie diet and regular exercise can significantly improve symptoms, increase fertility, and manage blood sugar levels.', 'Hormonal Birth Control: Pills, patches, or hormonal IUDs can regulate menstrual cycles, reduce acne, and lower androgen levels.', 'Insulin Sensitizers: Metformin is often prescribed to manage insulin resistance, weight gain, and Type 2 diabetes risk.', 'Fertility Medications: Medications like letrozole or clomiphene can induce ovulation.', 'Anti-Androgens: Medications like spironolactone can reduce hair growth and acne.', 'Hair Removal: Cosmetic procedures (laser, electrolysis) or creams can treat excessive facial/body hair.']
